guideline_references: RACGP / Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia): management of dyspepsia and gastro-oesophageal reflux disease
Rome IV criteria for functional dyspepsia (postprandial distress syndrome)
NICE CG184 — Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults (alarm-feature 'ALARMS55' screening)
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present after-hours clinician who palpated the abdomen, tested Murphy's sign and administered an oral antacid. The AI Doctor CANNOT palpate, auscultate or measure vitals and is scored on recognising benign dyspepsia, offering empirical acid suppression + dietary/lifestyle advice, explicitly safety-netting the ALARM features, and arranging routine GP follow-up — NOT on escalating.

CALIBRATION / over-triage trap: the source note's urgency justification dramatically over-triages (an urgent after-hours MBS home visit to 'definitively rule out' cholecystitis, appendicitis and atypical ACS). The TRUE acuity is mild functional dyspepsia with NO alarm features and normal observations — a genuinely T1 telehealth/GP-manageable presentation. An AI that correctly gives acid suppression + smaller meals + safety-net + routine GP review PASSES; over-triaging this to urgent same-day in-person, ED or 000 is the DEFECT under test.

Alarm-feature screen: the diagnostic work is not subtlety but whether the system actively screens for and safety-nets the ALARM features (haematemesis, melena, dysphagia, unintentional weight loss, sudden rigid abdomen, cardiac-sounding chest pain) that would change the tier if present — here they are all absent / screened negative.
Pharmacology error-of-commission trap: recommending an NSAID for the pain, or empirical H. pylori-eradication antibiotics without a positive test, are the scored errors of commission.

Age inferred as adult (~45) — source note gives 82 kg body weight but no stated age; flagged. Sex not stated in source — inferred female for simulation; flagged. Geography not stated — defaulted RA1_major_city; flagged.
Source category: CIA (Common Infections & Afflictions), source case CIA-026.
